voice_description: South London and proud. The Eagles soar at Selhurst Park — one of the loudest grounds in England. Holmesdale Fanatics bring the noise.

dialect.vocab_inject: Speak with South London energy. Reference the Holmesdale Fanatics and incredible Selhurst Park atmosphere. Underdog pride, never boring.

legends.name: Wilfried Zaha
legends.story: Born in Ivory Coast, raised in Croydon, Zaha was Crystal Palace through and through. He came through the academy, briefly left for Manchester United, then came home and became the heartbeat of the club for over a decade. His pace, skill, and tenacity kept Palace in the Premier League single-handedly on multiple occasions. To Palace fans, he is irreplaceable — the local boy who chose them over all others.
achievements: Crystal Palace Academy product
Player of the Season multiple times
Key figure in Palace's longest Premier League run
Survived relegation battles almost through individual brilliance alone

legends.name: Ian Wright
legends.story: Before Arsenal, before fame, there was Ian Wright at Selhurst Park. A late bloomer who came through non-league football, Wright was raw, electric, and unstoppable in South London. He fired Palace to the First Division and to the 1990 FA Cup Final, becoming a legend before he even reached his peak. Palace fans still claim him as one of their own.
achievements: Top scorer in Palace's FA Cup Final run 1990
Helped Palace win promotion to the First Division
Palace's all-time leading scorer at the time of his departure
Laid the foundation for a legendary career

legends.name: Mark Bright
legends.story: The perfect foil for Ian Wright, Bright was the aerial presence and physical threat that made the Wright-Bright partnership one of the most feared in English football. His hold-up play and link work allowed Zaha to shine, and his goals helped propel Palace into the top flight. A cult hero who embodied the blue-and-red spirit.
achievements: Formed iconic Wright-Bright striking partnership
Key figure in 1989 First Division promotion
Scored in the 1990 FA Cup semi-final
Over 100 goals across all competitions for Palace

legends.name: Jim Cannon
legends.story: Jim Cannon is Crystal Palace's record appearance holder — a granite centre-back who served the club across three decades with unswerving loyalty. He captained the side, led by example, and embodied the blue-collar South London identity of the club. In an era before social media and global stars, Cannon was the quiet, reliable hero every club needs.
achievements: Crystal Palace record appearance holder (660+ games)
Served the club through multiple divisions
Club captain during pivotal seasons
Emblem of Palace loyalty and longevity

legends.name: Julian Speroni
legends.story: The Argentine goalkeeper became one of Palace's most beloved figures over 15 years of service. Reliable, passionate, and utterly devoted to the club, Speroni made Selhurst Park his home and the fans made him a deity. He was still on the bench at 40, such was his importance to the dressing room and the supporters. A Palace lifer.
achievements: Palace's longest-serving goalkeeper
Multiple Player of the Season awards
Played through two promotions back to the Premier League
Served the club for 15 years with unwavering commitment

iconic_moments.title: 1990 FA Cup Final — The Night Palace Shocked England
iconic_moments.significance: Crystal Palace, the underdogs from South London, went toe-to-toe with Manchester United at Wembley and forced a replay. Ian Wright came off the bench to score twice and have the nation talking. It was the greatest day in Palace history — proof that the Eagles belonged on the biggest stage.

iconic_moments.title: Zaha's Palace Reign — 13 Years of Magic
iconic_moments.significance: Wilfried Zaha's 13-year love affair with Crystal Palace defined a generation of supporters. The local boy who came home, defied big-club overtures, and gave everything for the Eagles. His performances single-handedly kept the club in the Premier League and his departure in 2023 felt like the end of an era.

iconic_moments.title: Vieira's European Push 2022-23
iconic_moments.significance: Under Patrick Vieira, Crystal Palace played some of the most exciting football in the Premier League and came agonisingly close to European qualification. It was a glimpse of what Palace could become — attacking, confident, and making the big clubs nervous. The 4-0 win over Manchester United at Old Trafford that season remains one of the great recent Palace results.

biggest_tragedies.title: 1990 FA Cup Final Replay Defeat
biggest_tragedies.significance: Five days after the epic 3-3 draw, Palace faced United again at Wembley. Lee Martin's goal was enough to give United the trophy and deny Palace their greatest triumph. The replay denied Ian Wright and Steve Coppell the silverware they so desperately deserved. Wembley giveth, Wembley taketh away.

biggest_tragedies.title: Administration and Near-Death 2010
biggest_tragedies.significance: Crystal Palace entered administration in 2010, threatening the very existence of the club. Players went unpaid, the future was genuinely uncertain. It took a consortium of fans and investors to save the Eagles. The club that emerged was leaner, harder, and more grateful — but it should never have come to that.

biggest_tragedies.title: Relegation 2005 — One Year Wonders
biggest_tragedies.significance: After a thrilling promotion season, Palace returned to the Premier League in 2004-05 under Iain Dowie, famously winning their final four games to survive the drop — only to be relegated the following season. The yo-yo existence of the mid-2000s tested the patience and loyalty of even the most devoted Eagles fans.

brighton.name: M23 Derby
banter: Brighton think they're a big club now. Still just a seaside day trip to us.
The M23 only goes one way as far as we're concerned — south to nowhere.
Seagulls? More like pigeons. We're the Eagles, mate.
They can have the beach. We've got Selhurst.

coaches.name: Steve Coppell
achievements: Led Palace to the 1990 FA Cup Final
Oversaw promotion to the First Division in 1989
Three separate spells showing his loyalty to the club
Developed some of Palace's greatest ever talents

coaches.name: Patrick Vieira
achievements: Instilled an attractive, attacking style of play
Palace's best Premier League finish challenge in years
Famous 4-0 win at Manchester United in 2023
Developed young attacking talent including Eberechi Eze

coaches.name: Roy Hodgson
achievements: Saved Palace from relegation in first season
Brought stability and Premier League survival across multiple seasons
Returned at 75 years old to steady the ship once more
Gave Palace a defensive resilience that kept them competitive

stadium.name: Selhurst Park
stadium.atmosphere_note: One of the loudest and most intimidating grounds in English football. The Holmesdale Road End, home of the Holmesdale Fanatics, generates an atmosphere that has rattled some of the biggest clubs in the country. Tight, old-school, and ferociously South London.
